Preprocessing: high pass filter (12th order Butterworth at 1 kHz); low pass filter (12th order Butterworth at 16kHz)
DetectorDetails: trigger frequency filter for click detector module: 2 kHz threshold (high pass 6th order Butterworth); Basic Click Detector parameters were kept at default values
ClassifierDetails: PAMGuard classified details in Appendix Table B4 of paper; R script filtering based on click rate in 10 or 30s period
ManualReview: all recordings labeled with “presence” visually and aurally assessed using Adobe Audition

ClassifierDetails: NA
Comments: PAMguard click detector also used, but as described in Miller and Miller 2018

ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: examine 10 s spectrograms (FFT 1024, 0.5 overlap), 0 to 30 kHz frequency range and listening to sections of interest

ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: examine 10 s spectrogram (FFT 2000, 75% overlap, bandwidth 40 kHz)

ClassifierDetails:
DL: Macaulay, 2020
CNN: uses 5s spectrograms (viewing 1.2-19.1 kHz), implemented in Python, using PyTorch
PerformanceDetails:
PAMGuard click detector alone: 0.452 false positive rate
MHT: accuracy >= 82.6%; FDR <= 0.11 for sites used for training
DL: accuracy >= 79.4%; FDR <= 0.12 for sites used for training
For both, performance drops at new site not included in training data, with high false detection rates (more details in text)
ManualReview:
Trained on manually annotated data
DL: required additional annotations to train the CNN step
Comments:
DL: Writing and implementing requires a higher level of programming skill, which may be costly, and time consuming for a novice; classification cut-off vlaue manually determined (e.g. to limit FDR) and optimal value varies depending on site noise levels
MHT: more accessible, within PAMGuard; can make it more attractive to those unable to invest effort into developing their own DL algorithms

DetectorDetails:
LTSA: 30m window; resolution: 5 s; 100 Hz
SPICE detector: 10dB SNR threshold, duration 25-1200us, removed isolated clicks (>10s) & echos (<0.001s)
ClassifierDetails: unsupervised machine learning algorithm
ManualReview: ensured that these sperm whale clicks were not confused with ship passages by  inspecting the full spectrograms around the time of the click encounters and removing any detections that occurred  concurrently with ship passages

Preprocessing: high pass filter (400 Hz); resampled to 288 kHz sampling rate
DetectorDetails: LTSA: 1h window (to detect cetacean signals that were missed in the review of the impulsive signals)
ClassifierDetails: click classification function within the click detection module then click template classifier within PAMGuard Viewer
ManualReview: An experienced acoustic analyst reviewed results in 2-minute time slices in the click detector’s amplitude/time display
Comments: Multi-species (beaked whales, sperm whale, delphinid); only 1 SW detection event

ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: examine 5 s spectrogram (FFT 1000, 0% overlap)

Preprocessing: low pass filter (4th order Butterworth at 1 kHz) [this doesn't sound right - maybe they meant high pass?]
ClassifierDetails: considered sperm whales present when > 300 clicks with ICI of 0.9-1.5s per hr
PerformanceDetails:
true positive rate: 96%; false positive rate: 1%; precision: 83.3% (hourly; for 'nearby' sperm whales) 
Area under the receiver-operator characteristic curve (AUROC): 0.928 (all sperm whales)
Precision for the full dataset (not just 150 text hours): 0.68 (i.e. 32% of detections were false positives)
ManualReview: 150 hours of data were visually and aurally inspected blind for calssifier validation. All hours with positive detections were looked at by an expert to validate true positives and remove false positives

DetectorDetails: manually tuned the parameters to maximize the chances of detecting sperm whale clicks under a range of background noise scenarios
ClassifierDetails: searched for ICIs of 0.5-2s, rounded to the same 0.25s, with 6 repetitions
PerformanceDetails:
setting the repetition threshold significantly affected performance at daily resolution
At optimal: precision: 96%, recall: 96%, false positive rate: 4%
ManualReview: The optimal value for repetition threshold was determined via comparison to manual identification of sperm whale search clicks

ClassifierDetails: classifies codas, so not super relevant here
ManualReview: not described
Comments: did not specify whether other species or noise sources were included in their training or testing data and "potential source of classification error could be derived from label noise in the training dataset, which should be addressed in a more comprehensive study investigating CNN-based click detectors".

Preprocessing: bandpass filter (5-95 kHz); received level >= 130 dBpp
ClassifierDetails: Removed other odontocete clicks based on spectral characteristics; detected ship passages using an automated classifier based on average power spectral densities and removed clicks during passages
ManualReview: Manually removed the remaining false positives using DetEdit

ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: examine 10 s spectrogram (FFT 2000, 75% overlap, bandwidth 40 kHz)

ClassifierDetails: default spectrum templates for delphinids, sperm whales and beaked whales used to classify clicks within trains
ManualReview: Manually verified sections identified by the detectors as containing potential odontocete presence. Click trains manually marked in PAMGuard Viewer Mode

Preprocessing: bandpass filter (4th order Butterworth 2-20 kHz); decimated to 96 kHz
DetectorDetails: general sperm whale click detector with a trigger threshold of 12 dB
ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: all detections were reviewed to classify click types and mark click trains as “events”

DetectorDetails: All detections in a 40-degree sector ahead of the vessel were discarded to reduce false detections from vessel noise; 16dB detection threshold (determined though testing multiple thresholds)
ClassifierDetails: Used to  automatically group detected clicks into click trains
PerformanceDetails: 86/636 true pos; no false neg (at click train level)
ComputationCost:
1696 hr of .wav files took ~353 hr (4.8x real-time);
Classier is 'computationally intensive'; removing as many false positive clicks as possible before running  MHT proved essential; standard laptop took 22.3 hr to run MHT on 1696 hr of data
ManualReview: All sections of data with click train detections were manually audited using PAMGuard viewer

ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: examine 5 s spectrogram (FFT 1000, 0% overlap)

DetectorDetails: custom click detection algorithm similar to that used in PAMGuard
ClassifierDetails: scores clicks as good or bad based on presence or absence of clear multipulsed waveforms characteristic of on-axis clicks
PerformanceDetails: true positive rate: 89.1%, true negative rate: 99.5%, precision: 92.7%
ManualReview: not described
Comments: trained to expect data in 2-12kHz - may perform poorly with recordings with narrower frequency range; computes IPIs; proportion of detected clicks accepted is always very low

ClassifierDetails: NA
PerformanceDetails: "achieves low false alarm rates in all cases without the need for pre-training in this environment and with little variation between data sources for threshold setting" (<=6%)
ManualReview: not described

DetectorDetails: 1 h window; resolution: 5 s (500 spectra, 10 ms Hann-windowed frames); 100 Hz
ClassifierDetails: NA
ManualReview: When echolocation signals were notable in the LTSA the sequence was inspected more closely

DetectorDetails: can provide detections, or use simple energy detector, in tandem with LSTA
ClassifierDetails: Thresholds can be defined for peak frequency, transformed RLrms and RLpp to automate the process of annotating data
ComputationCost: A total of 202 TB of data (with 34 million sperm whale clicks) was verified and corresponding false positive rates were calculated
ManualReview: User can parse the data by selecting single detections to visualize the features

DetectorDetails: detect odontocete clicks containing energy within the 2 kHz to 150 kHz band
ClassifierDetails: a visual identification of click trains on the time-bearing display of a PAMGuard detector was found to be more reliable than classifiers
ManualReview: Two experienced analysts independently identified and ‘marked-up’ sperm whale click sequences thought to originate from the same individual or from a small group.

ClassifierDetails: cross correlation of  clicks' normalised average spectrum to a species-specific spectrum template
ManualReview: Click trains marked for testing algorithms
